Â Â Per Clausen (The Left). â Fru formand! NÃ¥r luftfart og kritisk infrastruktur i flere EU-lande er blevet forstyrret af uidentificerede droner og cyberangreb, sÃ¥ viser det Ã©n ting for mig: EU og mit eget land Danmark er ikke klar til at imÃ¸degÃ¥ de reelle trusler, vi stÃ¥r over for. Det er der flere grunde til. En af dem er, at man har vÃ¦ret mere fokuseret pÃ¥ konkurrenceevne og deregulering og blind oprustning end pÃ¥ at lÃ¸se de reelle problemer. Europa stÃ¥r jo lige nu reelt over for tre store trusler, som alle angriber vores sikkerhed. Det er klimakrisen, det er en voksende social ulighed og fattigdom, der underminerer vores sammenhÃ¦ngskraft, og sÃ¥ er det trusler og hybridangreb fra fremmede magter. Og er vi Ã¦rlige, sÃ¥ betyder det jo sidst og ikke mindst truslen fra Rusland, men ogsÃ¥ USA, som jo truer Danmark og GrÃ¸nland. Og her er er det vigtigt at vÃ¦re klare. Den sÃ¥kaldte dronemur lyder mÃ¥ske smart, men det er nÃ¦ppe lÃ¸sningen. Vi kan jo se, hvordan droner kan komme alle steder fra og ikke respekterer mure eller grÃ¦nser. LÃ¸sningen er heller ikke at kÃ¸be flere dyre amerikanske jagerfly og missiler. I stedet har vi behov for et styrket civilforsvar, styrket luftforsvar og for beskyttelse af vores kritiske infrastruktur og for Ã©n gang for alle at stoppe den skyggeflÃ¥de, som med hÃ¸jst sandsynlighed bruges til at lancere en del af de droner, der har skabt os sÃ¥ mange problemer.

Â 
Â Â

Â Â Tom Berendsen (PPE). â Voorzitter, collegaâs, Rusland test systematisch de bescherming van onze kritieke infrastructuur, maar ook van binnenuit ontstaat een bedreiging. Vorige week werd ik gastvrij ontvangen in de havens van Gdynia en GdaÅsk in Polen. Beide havens liggen binnen een straal van honderd kilometer van de Russische grens bij Kaliningrad. Dagelijks zijn er zorgen over de verstoring van gpsâsignalen, de Russische schaduwvloot, de veiligheid op de Baltische Zee en onbekende drones.

Maar er is meer. Een groot deel van de haven van Gdynia, die van cruciaal belang is voor de aanvoer van militair materieel richting de oostflank van Europa, is in handen van Hutchison, een bedrijf dat onder controle van China staat. Deze haven is geen alleenstaand geval, want de Chinese invloed is groot in tal van belangrijke Europese havens. Daarom is de aangekondigde Europese havenstrategie van groot belang. Die moet ervoor zorgen dat we de sleutels van onze havens, de poorten naar Europa, terugkrijgen en op die manier onze kritieke infrastructuur weer kunnen beschermen.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Tonino Picula (S&D). â Madam President, Commissioner, colleagues, I strongly condemn the recent Russian provocations that serve only to stir divisions and test our capabilities. I have full understanding for all our Member States affected. I also believe that these innovative methods of provocation are a tool used in the same way as disinformation, started through social media some ten years ago.

However, these incidents are also a sign of the times â times in which we, as the EU, need to stand together, being aware of our differences, but primarily taking into account the many issues and aspects that keep us together.

Colleagues, we must not over-engage in these provocation games, but at the same time, we cannot stay idle and let them pull all their stunts. We have a commitment to our citizens to respond firmly and decisively. That is the only way Putin's regime can understand and respect our message.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Sebastian Tynkkynen (ECR). â Arvoisa puhemies, VenÃ¤jÃ¤ kÃ¤y jÃ¤rjestelmÃ¤llistÃ¤ hybridisotaa Eurooppaa vastaan. Informaatio-operaatioita, salamurhia, kyberiskuja, siirtolaisten vÃ¤lineellistÃ¤mistÃ¤, kriittisen infrastruktuurin sabotaasia, elektronista sodankÃ¤yntiÃ¤ ja GPS-hÃ¤irintÃ¤Ã¤.

Tuoreet ilmatilaloukkaukset ovat jopa VenÃ¤jÃ¤n mittapuulla poikkeuksellisen rÃ¶yhkeitÃ¤. VenÃ¤lÃ¤ishÃ¤vittÃ¤jien ilmatilaloukkaus Viroon oli syvyydeltÃ¤Ã¤n ja kestoltaan vakavin maan Nato-jÃ¤senyyden aikana. Puolaan kohdistunut droonihyÃ¶kkÃ¤ys vaati sotilaallista vÃ¤liintuloa. Droonihavainnot huolestuttavat myÃ¶s muissa maissa.

Kun VenÃ¤jÃ¤ testaa rajojamme, ei ole varaa epÃ¤rÃ¶intiin. VenÃ¤jÃ¤ on hyÃ¶kkÃ¤yssotaa kÃ¤yvÃ¤ terroristivaltio, joka uskoo vain voimaa. Euroopan maiden tulee puolustaa rajojaan pÃ¤Ã¤ttÃ¤vÃ¤isesti. Vihollisen droonit ja hÃ¤vittÃ¤jÃ¤t on tarvittaessa ammuttava alas. EmmekÃ¤ saa unohtaa: paras tae rauhalle Euroopassa on Ukrainan voitto.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Petras AuÅ¡treviÄius (Renew). â MadamÂ President, Commissioner, Minister, colleagues, Europe cannot allow itself to become a testing ground for Russian hybrid attacks, drones and provocations that undermine our security and economy and spread fear and terror among our population.

We must not surrender our democratic space to those who encroach upon it. We must act decisively and in unity. Let's recognise that we are lagging far behind in developing reliable defences against drones. We must make the most of Ukraine's experience and establish an anti-drone system through joint coordinated action now.

We should not concern ourselves with questions about whose drones are disrupting European airspace and endangering aviation. If these drones are not ours, we must treat them as hostile and immediately destroy them.

In order to effectively integrate drones and anti-drone technology into European security and defence, I call on European institutions to establish a regulatory framework that defines safety standards for civil aviation and critical infrastructure in the context of these new technologies.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Marta WcisÅo (PPE). â Pani PrzewodniczÄca! Panie Komisarzu! Rosyjskie drony naruszajÄ niebo Unii Europejskiej, Polski, takÅ¼e mojej Lubelszczyzny. To nie incydenty, to atak. Potrzebujemy europejskiej tarczy antydronowej, magazynÃ³w, efektywnej logistyki, inwestycji w âdual useâ, wspÃ³lnego dowodzenia i realnej obrony obywateli. ArtykuÅ 42 musi wreszcie dziaÅaÄ w praktyce, nie tylko na papierze. ZmarnowaliÅmy juÅ¼ zbyt wiele czasu. Nie moÅ¼emy czekaÄ, aÅ¼ kolejne drony spadnÄ na nasze domy, szkoÅy czy szpitale. Rosyjski imperializm trzeba zatrzymaÄ tu i teraz, natychmiast, z odwagÄ. Dlatego naleÅ¼y wykorzystaÄ rosyjskie zamroÅ¼one aktywa. Dlatego naleÅ¼y wzmocniÄ europejski przemysÅ obronny, takÅ¼e poprzez zniesienie ETS-u na produkcjÄ stali i chemii przemysÅowej, w imiÄ bezpieczeÅstwa 500 mln obywateli Unii Europejskiej. DoÅÄ czekania! Trzeba dziaÅaÄ! Panie Komisarzu, o to proszÄ.

Â

Â Â Juan Fernando LÃ³pez Aguilar (S&D). â SeÃ±ora presidenta, llevamos horas debatiendo una amenaza real: la creciente y reiterada violaciÃ³n de nuestro espacio aÃ©reo por drones rusos en una guerra hÃ­brida de Putin contra la UniÃ³n Europea y todo lo que la UniÃ³n representa.

Â¿Y quÃ© lecciones surgen despuÃ©s de haber escuchado a la presidenta Von der Leyen? La primera, la unidad. Claro que sÃ­. Y, la segunda, una estrategia para construir un muro europeo antidrones y un escudo europeo de defensa aÃ©rea y espacial. Pero no basta con enunciarla.

Desde luego, para que sea eficaz, no puede depender del incremento de veintisiete esfuerzos fragmentados, de veintisiete presupuestos nacionales. No. Es un ejemplo de libro de la necesidad de apuntar a una integraciÃ³n de nuestras capacidades de ciberseguridad con tecnologÃ­as sofisticadas que detecten y derriben drones hasta disuadir a Putin.

Pero ademÃ¡s, debe ser creÃ­ble. Y esto significa que no puede perjudicar lo que la UniÃ³n Europea realmente representa. La generaciÃ³n de inversiones y recursos requiere recursos propios e inversiones estratÃ©gicas de la propia UniÃ³n Europea y, por tanto, no puede perjudicar ni la polÃ­tica social ni de cohesiÃ³n, ni la solidaridad. Ni tampoco, por supuesto, ese pilar social del que depende la dignidad del trabajo y la vivienda que tanto preocupa a los europeos.

Â Â Vytenis Povilas Andriukaitis (S&D). â Madam President, dear Commissioner, we condemned â in the strongest terms â Putin's aggression against Ukraine and military and hybrid provocations against the EU and its Member States. The European Commission proposed: first, the protection of sovereignty and critical infrastructure of the European Union and its Member States; second, the creation of multi-layered anti-drone and air defence system; third, coordination with NATO; and fourth, to boost investment in the Security and Defence Union in a coordinated manner. I welcome those proposals â it is really a very strong statement.

But the report based on the research carried out for the European Parliamentary Research Services shows that the cost of non-EU in defence spending is estimated to range from EURÂ 18-57Â billion per year. I ask you, Mr Commissioner, to encourage Member States to understand that we need to spend every euro and to act in a coordinated manner. Do not allow Member States to act separately; the cost will be very high, much higher. We need the strongest united response.

Â Â Jaume Asens LlodrÃ  (Verts/ALE). â SeÃ±ora presidenta, llevamos cuatro horas. Cuatro horas hablando de cÃ³mo proteger el territorio europeo, sobre las posibles amenazas de Rusia. Â Â Liudas MaÅ¾ylis (PPE). â MadamÂ President, Commissioner, colleagues, the recent violations of Europe's airspace and critical infrastructure are not accidents and they are not mere provocations; they are deliberate acts of hostility. Russia is not intending to declare officially the war; the war simply is going on. We should try to avoid the fatal mistakes of the past. Today, history stands at our doorstep once again. War is raging on our continent and the integrity of borders is no longer an abstract principle. Europe's response must be immediate, coordinated and unmistakable. Our skies, our territory and our seas are not negotiable. Every act of aggression must be met with firm political, economic and, if necessary, military consequences.

Â Â Helmut BrandstÃ¤tter (Renew). â Frau PrÃ¤sidentin, Herr Kommissar! Wir haben heute in dieser Diskussion nicht nur erlebt, dass Putin uns mit hybridem Krieg bedroht, sondern auch, dass er hier Freunde hat und Marionetten sitzen hat.

Â Â Matej Tonin (PPE). â MadamÂ President, in today's debate, we are seeing how Russia is mocking and deliberately provoking the EU.Â Putin is doing so because we are a democracy and because he knows our rules of engagement.

Russia knows that NATO aircraft carrying out air policing can only shoot down Russian aeroplanes if NATO aircraft are attacked. The Russians are exploiting our rules of engagement so that they can use their propaganda machine to portray Europe as ineffective and slow.

If they want to attack the EU, then they have made a strategic mistake. They have wasted the element of surprise. When the moment of truth comes, Europe will be better prepared for Russian threats.

Â

Â Â Costas Mavrides (S&D). â MadamÂ President, here is a sample of wording from MsÂ von der Leyen's initial statement regarding the recent Russian drone incursion against our eastern borders: 'hybrid warfare', 'deliberate campaign against Europe', 'we want to respond with unity, with the drone wall and other actions.'

We fully agree with united actions to defend the EU. However, hundreds and hundreds of air, naval and terrain violations are happening by TÃ¼rkiye against our borders in the eastern Mediterranean. Right now, two Turkish armed drones are violating the Cyprus FIR, not to mention the continued occupation of EU territory in Cyprus.

The Commission is supposed to be the guardian of EU Treaties and values, not to divide us by silence, double standards or hypocrisies.

Â Â SeÃ¡n Kelly (PPE). â A UachtarÃ¡in, how the narrative changes. A few months ago, we were all dancing to the tune of Donald Trump as he touted his peace initiative in Ukraine. Of course, Russia had no interest whatsoever in peace, as we have learned since. Also, of course, talking about peace in the middle of a war is like a football team talking about celebrations in the middle of a match. The best way to guarantee celebrations is to win the match; the best way to guarantee peace is to win the war.

In fairness to the Ukrainians, they are doing a damn good job at it. A three day war, in Putin's eyes, has extended to three years and a lot longer. He has lost 1Â 111Â 000 troops and is not making great progress: a kilometre here or a kilometre there.

As regards the violations of our airspace, I have one observation: what would happen if we violated Russian or Belarusian airspace? There's our answer â you meet fire with fire. We have got to develop the firepower to do so and Commissioner Kubilius is doing his best to build up our defences. We need to rally around and make sure it happens.

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

(Ende der spontanen Wortmeldungen)

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Andrius Kubilius, Member of the Commission. â Madam President, Minister, dear colleagues, it was really a great debate, a great, important debate, because the issues that we are discussing are really very crucial. Many of the speakers were repeating very well-known truths â we are not at war, but not at peace either. What we need to recognise is that perhaps it will continue for years to come with provocations, disruptions and sabotage acts. So we are really under attack and we need to be ready for that. To defend and to deter in a united response, as in the name of this discussion.

What we need to do is clear; let us remember an ancient Roman saying â if you want peace, prepare for war, otherwise prepare for surrender if you want to have Putin-style peace. I would like to remind ourselves that it is Russia that waged a war against Ukraine, not Ukraine that attacked Russia. It is Russia that started provocations against Poland, the Baltic states, Copenhagen and other countries in Europe, not the EU that provoked Russia. In order to protect peace we need to ramp up our material defence readiness, first of all, which we are doing, but also our institutional defence readiness and our political defence readiness. Either we are politically and practically preparing to deter Putin or we are fighting each other. It is a choice for each of us.

Russia's provocations are reckless, irresponsible and dangerous, a threat to the people and peace of Europe. They must serve as a wake up call â we need to act very practically and effectively. Drones are the future of war and the future of provocations. It is a pity that there was a need for the recent provocations to wake us up, and it appears that we are badly prepared for this future. At the moment, our defences are really not ready to detect drones and to see and to track them when they are crossing our borders. In addition, shooting down a EURÂ 10Â 000 drone with a EURÂ 1Â million missile is really not sustainable. What we need to understand is that all of Europe is under threat â and I mean all of Europe. A Russian Geran-2 drone has a 2Â 500Â km range, and from Kaliningrad or Belarus it can hit targets in Denmark, Sweden, Belgium, Netherlands, Germany, northern France and Hungary, and it can even reach Brussels and Strasbourg. From container ships, drones can destroy targets all along the coast of Europe.

The question is: what needs to be done? There are a lot of things which we are doing and a lot of things which we need to do even more, but let me concentrate this time on drones and anti-drone capabilities. There is quite a broad agreement on what we need to do. In the short run, we need to develop capabilities to better detect, track and identify drones with acoustic sensors, radars, satellites. In the long run, capabilities to disarm or destroy drones, with drone interceptors, electronic warfare and mobile units. For the eastern flank countries there is also a need for capabilities to use the drones to hit the targets on the ground if an enemy tries to invade the country. A tailored approach, but that is what is needed. Frontline states have different needs compared to other countries, including countries in the Mediterranean, but all need capabilities to detect and to destroy drones.

Above all, what we need is cooperation, of course, to exchange data to track incursions across national borders. We have interoperable situational awareness to jointly identify targets and carry out operations. We will work hand in hand with NATO. We cannot do this without Ukraine; Ukraine's air defences take down 70â80Â % of targets, Ukrainian drones chased the Russian fleet out of the Black Sea, brought the Russian advance to a standstill and devastated the Russian strategic bomber fleet and oil refineries. In the development of the drone wall special attention should be paid to Ukrainian experience â this experience shows that the key to an effective drone wall is not just technology as such, but the developed skills of integrated command and the creation of a comprehensive new warfare ecosystem.

We must move quickly from ideas to actions. It is possible during the next year to build essential capabilities of the drone wall and by 2030 we shall have the possibility to spend EURÂ 2.5Â trillion for different needs for our defence. I hope that frozen assets will be used to strengthen Ukrainian defence very soon; so there are financial means. I count on the support of this House with the ideas and to projects which we need to develop and are ready to work very intensively with the Parliament in order to rapidly develop needed capabilities. Thanks a lot.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Marie Bjerre, President-in-Office of the Council. â Madam President, honourable Members, Commissioner, there have been worrying comments in this debate, but this is probably also the most positive and hopeful debate I participated in. It might sound like a paradox, because yes, Russia's actions are part of a serious escalation, and Europe is in a crucial time.

Let us be clear: Russia will not stop. Therefore, we need strong unity and a strong response, and that is exactly why I get hopeful when listening to this debate today. It illustrates what is happening: Europe stands more together.

While Danes like Mr Vistisen from the Patriots criticise Denmark for receiving assistance last week during our informal European Council meeting in Copenhagen, it is not a sign of weakness. Denmark received police assistance from Sweden and Norway and military assistance from Germany and France. It is a display of unity. And what is about to happen? Europe stands more together. We need to show solidarity and action, as several of you said. That is what we do.

I agree with the comments from several of you â not all, but the debate shows broad political support for a stronger Europe and a stronger response. MrÂ Halicki from EPP was advocating for a stronger defence industry in Europe. MsÂ GarcÃ­a PÃ©rez from S&D said that Ukraine needs to win. MsÂ Hayer from Renew said that we must be powerful if we want to be free. MsÂ Reintke from the Greens said that we need European strength.

All these comments, and many more, show that the EU is getting better prepared â that we stand more united. But we must continue the course. We need to enhance our support for Ukraine to defend itself against Russia's war of aggression. This requires state-of-the-art technology, and Ukraine is a key partner in this regard. We need to learn from their battlefield experience and foster close collaboration on the defence industry with Ukraine, including on EU soil.

At the same time, we must continue to maximise pressure on Russia. This requires urgently strengthening the restrictive measures against Russia and stepping up our outreach efforts to other countries.

Last but not least, we need to ensure accountability for Russia's continued war of aggression in Ukraine. Russia's war crimes cannot be left unpunished.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Die PrÃ¤sidentin. â Vielen Dank, Frau Ministerin! Vielen Dank auch allen Kollegen, die an dieser sehr wichtigen Debatte teilgenommen haben! Es waren sehr viele in der heutigen Debatte. Vielen Dank dafÃ¼r.

Zum Abschluss der Aussprache wurden gemÃ¤Ã ArtikelÂ 136 AbsatzÂ 2 der GeschÃ¤ftsordnung sechs EntschlieÃungsantrÃ¤ge eingereicht.

Die Aussprache ist geschlossen.

Die Abstimmung findet morgen, Donnerstag, 9.Â Oktober 2025, statt.

Die Sitzung ist nun kurz unterbrochen. Sie wird in wenigen Minuten mit der Ansprache von Jens-Frederik Nielsen, dem Premierminister GrÃ¶nlands, wieder aufgenommen. Vielen Dank.

Schriftliche ErklÃ¤rungen (Artikel 178)

Â 
Â Â 
Â 		Â 

Â Â Wouter Beke (PPE), schriftelijk. â Mevrouw Von der Leyen,

Beste collegaâs,

Rusland test en provoceert. Het wil zien hoe ver het kan gaan om onze eenheid op de proef stellen en tegelijk de aandacht afleiden van OekraÃ¯ne. We mogen ons daar niet door laten uit elkaar spelen. We moeten het hoofd koel houden en kordaat reageren met Ã©Ã©n gezamenlijk Europees antwoord.

En daartoe kwam u met concrete initiatieven binnen de Defence Readiness Roadmap. De discussie over deze of gene naam is daarbij onbelangrijk: het maakt niet uit of de kat wit of zwart is, als ze maar muizen vangt.

Wat telt, is dat we samenwerken met OekraÃ¯ne, dat intussen de ervaring Ã©n technologie heeft om Russische drones efficiÃ«nt te onderscheppen. Hun kennis moeten we samenleggen met alle hoogtechnologische industrie die in Europa al voor handen is om onze eigen luchtverdediging te versterken.

Ik vraag u dan ook om snel werk te maken van uw initiatieven, in samenwerking met onze industrie en de NAVO, maar bovenal met onze OekraÃ¯ense partners. De tijd om hiermee te talmen is voorbij, ik geloof in uw daadkracht.

Ik dank u.

ÎÎ¯Î½Î±Î¹ Î¿ Î¼ÏÎ½Î¿Ï ÏÏÏÏÎ¿Ï Î³Î¹Î± Î½Î± Î±ÏÎ¿Î´ÎµÎ¯Î¾Î¿ÏÎ¼Îµ Î±ÏÏÏ ÏÎ¿Ï Î¼Î±Ï Î¶Î·ÏÎ¿ÏÎ½ Î¿Î¹ ÎÏÏÏÏÎ±Î¯Î¿Î¹ ÏÎ¿Î»Î¯ÏÎµÏ, ÏÎ·Î½ Î±Î»Î»Î·Î»ÎµÎ³Î³ÏÎ· ÏÏÎ·Î½ ÏÏÎ¬Î¾Î·.

Â Â 
Â Â 

(Die Sitzung wird um 11:58 Uhr unterbrochen.)

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

IN THE CHAIR: ROBERTA METSOLA
President

Â 

4. Resumption of the sitting
Video of the speeches
Â Â 

(The sitting resumed at 12:12)

Â 

5. Formal sitting â Address by Jens-Frederik Nielsen, Prime Minister of Greenland
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â President. â It is an honour to welcome the Prime Minister of Greenland, Jens-Frederik Nielsen, to the European Parliament today. This is the first time that a leader of Greenland has ever addressed this House. A truly historic moment and a milestone in the partnership between Greenland and the European Union.

Jens-Fredrik, welcome. Tikilluarit. And thank you for being with us. You stand here today on behalf of all the people of Greenland â a people with a proud history and a strong future.

This Parliament knows it. And our message to you is this: when you look to Europe, you will always find an outstretched hand, a partner and a friend that stands with you in mutual respect. The ties between Greenland and the European Union run deep and they continue to grow. They have been shaped by the history that we share, by the challenges that we face together and by the way we see the world around us.

This House is grateful for the strong support for Greenland shown by Denmark in uneasy moments. It has led an EU drive of renewed partnership.

We meet at a time of uncertainty. The security and stability that we once took for granted can no longer be assumed. This was the clear message from European leaders in Copenhagen last week. And that is why, with Greenland as a strategic partner, particularly with Denmark holding the EU Presidency, we are working side by side to strengthen stability across the Arctic, to counter hybrid threats and to tackle growing security risks.

But our partnership goes far beyond security. It is about people, about making sure that communities, families and businesses in Greenland and in Europe can look to the future with confidence. That is why young people are at the heart of our partnership and that is why the European Union is investing in education and training in Greenland, with more than EUR 200 million already committed and more support on the way. This investment gives young Greenlanders the skills, the tools and the confidence to shape their own future and determine their own path.

So, dear Prime Minister, dear Jens-Frederik, thank you for being with us today. We look forward to hearing what you have to say, and to continuing our partnership in the years ahead. The floor is yours.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Jens-Frederik Nielsen, Prime Minister of Greenland. â (The speaker spoke Greenlandic)

Your excellency, President Metsola, and honourable Members of the European Parliament, I am truly honoured for the invitation to address you, the parliamentarians of the European Union, here today. As you are the democratic voices of all the people in the European Union, I am eager to share with you my vision of Greenland's place in the evolving EU-Greenland partnership, a partnership that is of great importance to us.

2025 has been an eventful, if not dramatic, year for Greenland. The world is changing and it's changing fast. Greenland is no stranger to change. Our people, the Greenlandic people â Kalaallit â is a proud people who has survived and thrived in the harsh conditions of the Arctic for thousands of years. We have lived off the land and the ocean while respecting it and not taking more than was necessary. We have adapted our livelihoods to the climate and learned to recognise and react to the changes in our environment.

As we have dealt with these changes, one thing has remained constant: the recognition that we need each other. We need each other to deal with changes, both the challenges and opportunities. We would not have been able to survive if every member of our people did not contribute and fulfil a specific role. The country of Greenland is built on cooperation and the desire to cooperate based on fundamental values such as equality and respect for one another. This continues to be the core of Greenland today, also in our relations with the world outside of Greenland. We need cooperation and partnerships with like-minded countries and institutions who share our values.

The EU has been a stable, reliable and important partner for Greenland for more than 40 years. It has evolved from a cooperation primarily focused on fisheries to now including education, raw material, energy, biodiversity, research and climate change. Our relationship has gradually grown and expanded throughout the years, most recently with the EU office opening in Greenland, which has been a great tool for cooperation and connection.

The EU has been a steadfast friend and has stood by us when we needed it the most. I would like to extend a heartfelt thank you to you, the EU, for your strong support for Greenland during the challenging times we are going through right now.

You have stood by us as a friend and partner. We will never forget this. Thank you.

(Applause)

As we look ahead, we wish to continue to strengthen and develop our cooperation and partnership with the EU. As the world is changing fast, new challenges are presented. I believe that Greenland can offer solutions for the EU, and the EU can offer solutions for Greenland, creating new opportunities for us both on mutually beneficial terms.

The recent presentation of the European Commission's draft for the multiannual financial framework was followed closely by Greenland. We noted with great enthusiasm the presentation of the draft decision on overseas association, including Greenland. We were very happy to see the suggested focus areas for the cooperation with Greenland, namely education, critical minerals, renewable energy, digitalisation and sustainable tourism. These are all important areas where there is great potential to strengthen and develop our cooperation even further â areas that can be beneficial for both Greenland and the EU.

We were also very encouraged to see that EURÂ 530Â million is suggested to be allocated for cooperation with Greenland. We know that there will be long negotiations ahead where also you, as parliamentarians, will play key roles, but the suggested amount from the Commission is promising in terms of further developing our cooperation. This could, for example, include investments in the renewable energy sector and mineral resources sector, including infrastructure investment in different mining projects.

Our emerging mineral sector is poised to become a key player in securing supply lines of strategic and critical raw materials to the EU and to the world. Greenland holds 24 of the 34 critical minerals identified by the EU.

Developing mining projects is, however, capital-intensive. If we are to succeed in the exploitation of the critical minerals, there is a need to reduce the risk that investors take on. This task of reducing risks must partly be solved politically and not only by the markets.

In 2023, the EU and Greenland agreed on a strategic partnership on sustainable raw materials value chains. The objective of this partnership is to support the development of the mineral resources in Greenland, while also establishing raw materials value chains for the green transition in the EU. This partnership is a good and important step in our cooperation, and there are examples of projects where the EU has provided support. This is very positive.

Taking the current security situation in the world into account and the green transition, there is, however, a need to move faster. The critical minerals of Greenland have the potential to shift global and security balances, but action needs to be taken fast. Greenland is ready to move with the EU to hasten the pace.

It is not just our raw materials that are important for the security balances and the green transition; Greenland has the potential to become a key partner to the EU on renewable energy. Greenland's hydropower potential is both vast and untapped. With our abundant water resources, including glaciers and rivers, we are an ideal location for large-scale hydroelectric power projects. We have the potential to become a global leader in the export of green energy. This will create new income streams for Greenland and make a difference on a global scale. We are actively seeking investors for these projects, which are being offered in tenders next year. The EU Commission has already supported us in our first tender process and we are ready to collaborate further.

Greenland and our national utility company are currently in dialogue with the European Investment Bank to expand the hydropower plant near the capital, Nuuk. The engagement of the EIB in Greenland requires a framework agreement. Such an agreement is currently being negotiated between the relevant parties. Once the agreement is in place, we expect the EIB will be able to engage in other projects related to both infrastructure and raw materials. We look forward to exploring projects for such a collaboration further.

As we seek to strengthen our cooperation with the EU and other like-minded countries and institutions, connectivity becomes a key word. In today's world, no country and no economy can function and develop without strong connectivity.

In a country characterised by vast distances, dispersed settlements and harsh natural conditions, telecommunications are not merely a technical necessity â they are a lifeline. The digital network connects our communities and supports education, healthcare and business, and it's essential for both the well-being of our citizens and the emergency response capabilities of our authorities.

Greenland is connected internationally through satellites and two submarine cables. Internally, we rely on a combination of land-based radio stations, submarine cables and satellites. Some places, especially in the far north of our country, are very poorly served with sustainable and very slow internet connections. We therefore have a strong need to enhance and strengthen our digital connectivity. We need to close the digital divide in Greenland.

Under the EU's Connecting Europe Facility, Greenland's national telecommunications company has been successful in getting grants to conduct seabed surveys in preparation for enhancing our submarine cable infrastructure. We are very keen to explore more such possibilities with the EU to enhance Greenland's digital connection to Europe. This would also include strengthening the satellite connectivity, particularly to our northernmost regions.

It is essential for us that those who operate telecommunications in Greenland are trusted and reliable actors. This is vital to ensure the stability of the infrastructure and to prevent unauthorised parties from disrupting and compromising connections.

(Applause)

As we seek to enhance our connectivity, we are also focusing on our flight routes. In 2024, we opened a new international airport in our capital, Nuuk. In 2026, we plan to open another international airport in Ilulissat, 350 miles north of Nuuk, as well as a regional airport in Qaqortoq in South Greenland. Our new airports are expected to increase our international connectivity, allowing for further integration into international trade and tourism. This will be positive for our cooperation with important partners like the EU.

Greenland and the EU have a long-standing cooperation on trade, especially when it comes to our fish and shellfish. The fisheries relations between Greenland and the EU date back to 1985, when Greenland left the European Economic Community.

Fisheries agreements have been in place since and allowed EU vessels to fish in Greenlandic waters in return for a financial contribution. It also provides duty-free access for Greenlandic fisheries products to the EU markets. This, however, hinges on a satisfactory fisheries agreement.

Today, our fisheries cooperation is regulated in the sustainable fisheries partnership agreement, which continues to be important for both the EU and Greenland. It, however, also remains vital to continue to adjust the cooperation on fisheries to develop and grow the Greenlandic fishing industry, which is the most important industrial sector in Greenland. We expect that the fishing industry will continue to remain the most important economic driver for Greenland in the years to come. For this reason, we must be agile and ensure that our relations continue to be beneficial for both of us.

Greenland is a country of proud fishermen and hunters. Not only do fisheries continue to be vital for our economy, but it's also crucial for the subsistence of our communities and families. The proud tradition of seal hunting also remains crucial for our communities. Seal hunting is an important source of income and sustenance for many families. For some of our communities, it is a vital part of people's livelihoods.

Seal hunting is also an important part of the Inuit culture, with knowledge and skills being passed down through generations. In our words, it is the livelihood of our culture and our identity.

The general ban on placing seal products on the EU markets has had a grave negative impact on seal hunting and has led to significant drops in the domestic supply as well as in the export of sealskin â this all in spite of the exemption in the regulation for seal products derived from traditional harvests by Inuit and other indigenous peoples.

As the Commission takes the regulation up for evaluation in connection to the current fitness check, it is our sincere hope that the EU will consider and implement amendments that can alleviate the grave negative impact Greenland is experiencing from this ban. As we continue to strengthen and build on our relationship as friends and partners, we hope that we together can find a solution and a way forward for important issues such as this.

Our proud tradition of fisheries and hunting will continue to be a crucial part of the future of Greenland, with our youth standing ready to carry on the tradition. As we build and develop the future of Greenland, the education of our youth will be of utmost importance. The challenges and opportunities that are to come will require specific skills and knowledge to be able to handle.

Greenland and the EU have a strong and solid partnership in the field of education. It plays a significant economic role in the development of Greenland's education system and has opened doors for exchange of knowledge, resources and best practice. Through this cooperation, educational initiatives have been strengthened and the quality of teaching and learning in Greenland has been enhanced. We look forward to continuing to strengthen this important cooperation on education, which will ensure that Greenland remains a strong partner for the EU in the future.

As we look towards preparing a future for our youth and the following generations, one of the immediate challenges we are facing is the triple planetary crisis. This is a crisis that is putting Arctic ecosystems and populations under pressure. The Arctic is being transformed by accelerating climate change, transboundary pollution and biodiversity loss.

Through the cooperation between Greenland and the EU, important agreements and programmes addressing the triple planetary crises are being implemented in Greenland â this on a local and national level, but also in an international context.

As an example, the establishment of a new national park in West Greenland can be mentioned, which is being financed through the cooperation with the EU. The traditional use of this area and its valuable biodiversity are now accompanied by increasing interest from tourism operators and other industries. Climate change is impacting the area, as well as the pollution and waste from the increased level of activity. A management approach for the national park that balances the biological diversity of the area, traditional use and sustainable development is becoming a reality thanks to the EU.

Moving forward, we hope to do more cooperation such as this in order to address the challenges of the triple planetary crisis.

The avenues for cooperation between us are many. Some of them we have just started to explore together and others have been developed over several years. Our partnership has gradually changed to fit the needs of Greenland and the EU. I believe that we now stand in a pivotal moment of time where our partnership once again needs to adjust to the unique challenges and opportunities we are faced with. If there is one message that I hope you take from what I have said today, it is this: Greenland needs the European Union and the European Union needs Greenland.

(Applause)

(The speaker spoke Greenlandic)

I will say it in English now.

(Laughter and applause)

On behalf of the Government of Greenland, I wish to express our appreciation for the continued development of cooperation between Greenland and the European Union, which we believe contributes positively to the advancement of our society.

Thank you once again for your loyal support and for a reliable and strong partnership, and finally, thank you for being a trusted friend of Greenland.

Qujanaq â thank you.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â President. â Thank you very much Prime Minister. Your words truly remind us of the strength of the friendship between Greenland and the European Union. You are welcome back again anytime.

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

(The sitting was briefly suspended)

7.5. Guidelines for the employment policies of the Member States (A10-0160/2025 - Li Andersson) (vote)

Â 

7.6. Draft amending budget No 2/2025: update of revenue (own resources) and adjustments to expenditure (A10-0165/2025 - Victor Negrescu) (vote)
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â President. â The next vote is on the draft amending budget NoÂ 2/2025: update of revenue (own resources) and adjustments to expenditure (see minutes, item [7.6]).

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

â Before the vote:

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Victor Negrescu, rapporteur. â MadamÂ President, dear colleagues, this amending budget is not just about numbers. It's about supporting our farmers, protecting Europe's financial integrity and the credibility of the Union's finances.

We are about to vote on amending budget NoÂ 2 for 2025 â a technical but essential update that keeps the European Union's budget balanced and credible.

Unlike in our national systems, where an amending budget often means fixing imbalances, in the EU it means ensuring that both revenues and expenditures remain aligned. This adjustment reflects the realities of our economy and the progress of our programmes.

On the revenue side, the figures are slightly lower than forecast, especially for customs duties, VAT-based resources and the plastic waste levy. But, of course, this is actually good news, because it means Europeans are recycling more, proving that our sustainability efforts are working.

On the expenditure side, the most important change is the additional EURÂ 3.5Â billion for the rural development programmes. These are not just figures. They are direct investment in our farmers, in local communities and in the people who sustain Europe's food security. These funds, long overdue, are finally reaching the ground where they are needed the most.

We will also strengthen new areas of European governance, notably the establishment of the Anti-Money Laundering Authority, which will protect the integrity of our financial system and guarantee that EU money is clean, traceable and used for its true purpose.

To maintain a balanced budget, the GNI call rate will also be adjusted. It's a modest step, but one that keeps our finances sound and predictable.

At the same time, we urge the Commission to improve forecasting accuracy, accelerate the introduction of new own resources and ensure more flexibility in future budgets.

I therefore invite you to vote in favour of this report.

Â

10. Europeâs automotive future â reversing the ban on the sale of combustion cars in the EU (topical debate)
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â President. â The next item on the agenda is the topical debate requested by the political group ECR (RuleÂ 169) - Europe's automotive future - reversing the ban on the sale of combustion cars in the EU.

I would like to remind Members that for this debate, there is no catch-the-eye procedure and no blue-card questions will be accepted.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Patryk Jaki, autor. â Pani PrzewodniczÄca! Panie Komisarzu! Dyskusja na temat zakazu sprzedaÅ¼y samochodÃ³w spalinowych to jest dyskusja o czymÅ znacznie wiÄcej niÅ¼ tylko o przemyÅle. To jest dyskusja o wolnoÅci wyboru, ktÃ³ry rzekomo ma byÄ dla nas tak waÅ¼ny, czy wreszcie dyskusja o racjonalnoÅci podejmowanych decyzji i konkurencyjnoÅci.

Ale na poczÄtek powtÃ³rzmy: chcemy wycofania siÄ z najwiÄkszego oszustwa w historii, to znaczy z Zielonego Åadu, i wycofania siÄ z zakazu sprzedaÅ¼y samochodÃ³w spalinowych. Bo to, co robicie, to jest zwykÅe szaleÅstwo. I teraz tak: przemysÅ motoryzacyjny od dziesiÄcioleci stanowi jeden z najwiÄkszych filarÃ³w europejskiej gospodarki, zarÃ³wno pod wzglÄdem zatrudnienia, jak i eksportu, jak i badaÅ. Zatrudnia bezpoÅrednio i poÅrednio okoÅo 14 milionÃ³w osÃ³b. Odpowiada za 8% wartoÅci dodanej przemysÅu przetwÃ³rczego Unii Europejskiej i generuje nadwyÅ¼kÄ handlowÄ w wysokoÅci 120 mld euro rocznie. Likwidacja tego w imiÄ zielonej ideologii to katastrofa dla konkurencyjnoÅci przemysÅu europejskiego, miejsc pracy, bezpieczeÅstwa gospodarczego, struktury zatrudnienia oraz realnych moÅ¼liwoÅci technologicznych.

Porozmawiajmy najpierw o zaÅoÅ¼eniach. OtÃ³Å¼ planowaliÅcie, Å¼e zakaz sprzedaÅ¼y samochodÃ³w spalinowych w 2035 r. bÄdzie tylko punktem docelowym, a do tego czasu nakÅadaliÅcie nowe podatki i obciÄÅ¼enia na producentÃ³w samochodÃ³w spalinowych i dopÅacaliÅcie tym, ktÃ³rzy produkujÄ elektryki. I jaki mamy efekt? OtÃ³Å¼ w wyniku tego ceny nowych samochodÃ³w wzrosÅy o 30%. Jaki byÅ cel? Zmniejszenie emisji dwutlenku wÄgla i zmuszenie do zwyciÄstwa w rewolucji technologicznej i docelowo poprawa naszej pozycji konkurencyjnej. Tak miaÅo byÄ.

A jakie sÄ efekty? I siÄgnijmy teraz do waszego raportu, do raportu Draghiego. OtÃ³Å¼ z roku na rok nasza konkurencyjnoÅÄ zamiast rosnÄÄ, spada. W latach 2017â2022 eksport pojazdÃ³w z Unii Europejskiej spadÅ o 16%. W tym samym czasie import samochodÃ³w z Chin wzrÃ³sÅ piÄciokrotnie. ChiÅscy producenci kontrolujÄ juÅ¼ 14% rynku importowego w Unii Europejskiej, a udziaÅ europejskich marek w chiÅskiej sprzedaÅ¼y samochodÃ³w elektrycznych spadÅ o 6%. Od 2015 do 2023 r. udziaÅ chiÅskich marek w rynku pojazdÃ³w elektrycznych w Europie wzrÃ³sÅ z 5% do 15%, podczas gdy udziaÅ europejskich firm spadÅ z 80% do 60%. Koszty produkcji pojazdu elektrycznego obciÄÅ¼one Zielonym Åadem sÄ w Unii Europejskiej o 30% wyÅ¼sze niÅ¼ w Chinach, a tamtejsi producenci sÄ o jednÄ generacjÄ, jeÅ¼eli chodzi o zielone technologie, do przodu od nas. Dodatkowo kontrolujÄ okoÅo 70% globalnej podaÅ¼y ogniw litowo-jonowych, ktÃ³re sÄ konieczne w caÅym ÅaÅcuchu produkcji.

WiÄc gdyby porÃ³wnaÄ dzisiaj strategiÄ chiÅskÄ i amerykaÅskÄ, ktÃ³ra opieraÅa siÄ na stabilnych ÅºrÃ³dÅach energii i proporcjonalnym, spokojnym rozwijaniu OZE, do Unii Europejskiej, ktÃ³ra robiÅa to radykalnie â tamte strategie okazaÅy siÄ duÅ¼o skuteczniejsze. Tam niczego nie zakazujÄ, a w Stanach Zjednoczonych w ogÃ³le nie ma systemu ETS i wszystkich pochodnych i jakoÅ z nami wygrywajÄ. Do tego przez Zielony Åad mamy wyÅ¼sze ceny energii, pracy i surowcÃ³w, a to obniÅ¼a atrakcyjnoÅÄ inwestycji w ten sektor.

PostawiliÅcie sobie teÅ¼ cele klimatyczne i oczywiÅcie ja tylko pragnÄ przypomnieÄ, Å¼e one kompletnie nic nie dajÄ, dlatego Å¼e z roku na rok emisja dwutlenku wÄgla po prostu roÅnie. Mamy gigantyczny problem z Åadowaniem samochodÃ³w. Za kaÅ¼dym razem, kiedy samochÃ³d elektryczny jest Åadowany, w waszych tabelkach zawsze wynosi zero. Dlaczego nie rozmawiamy o tym, skÄd ta energia pochodzi? Ona bardzo czÄsto na przykÅad pochodzi z elektrowni wÄglowych, ale w tabelce u was zawsze wynosi zero. Albo nawet jeÅ¼eli pochodzi z odnawialnych ÅºrÃ³deÅ energii, to i tak musi byÄ zabezpieczona przez stabilne ÅºrÃ³dÅa energii, ktÃ³re sÄ emisyjne, bo inaczej byÅyby blackouty, tak jak mieliÅmy z tym do czynienia w Hiszpanii.

W Szwajcarii, jak PaÅstwo wiecie, byÅo juÅ¼ przygotowane nawet rozporzÄdzenie, Å¼e w przypadku braku energii naleÅ¼y zakazaÄ uÅ¼ytkowania samochodÃ³w elektrycznych. DokÅadnie to samo staÅo siÄ w Kalifornii w 2022 r. podczas upaÅÃ³w, kiedy wzywano mieszkaÅcÃ³w, aby unikali Åadowania samochodÃ³w elektrycznych. I generalnie to jest w ogÃ³le pytanie, dla kogo my wÅaÅciwie tworzymy ten system? To jest system, ktÃ³ry spowoduje, Å¼e samochÃ³d elektryczny stanie siÄ dobrem luksusowym.

(PrzewodniczÄca odebraÅa mÃ³wcy gÅos)

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Wopke Hoekstra, Member of the Commission. â MadamÂ President, honourable Members, Europe's industry has been at the forefront of technological progress for decades, and it has underpinned our economic prosperity and shaped our social market economy. Let me stress that that is especially true for our automotive industry. It is a truly fantastic sector which is, for the EU, the second-largest producer of motor vehicles in the world â behind China, but significantly ahead of the US. If you were to look at the trade surplus in motor vehicles, it has recently risen to almost EURÂ 100Â billion. It is truly phenomenal.

The sector also employs three million Europeans in direct and indirect manufacturing. But the automotive industry is also, of course, undergoing fundamental and profound change â towards zero emission and towards digitalisation. On top of that, the industry is facing fierce â and let me be very clear about it â unfair competition from outside the EU. It is also facing lower sales, higher costs of energy and supply chain dependencies. That all affects its competitiveness, and that is true throughout the value chain.

If that were not enough, the world is also going through huge geopolitical change and turmoil. All of this requires our full attention and our action, and we need to make sure we find smart and workable solutions. That is why we are continuing to support the sector, so that the automotive companies can not only survive, but can actually thrive on European soil.

President von der Leyen and Commissioners Tzitzikostas, SÃ©journÃ©, Roswall and myself have gathered automotive stakeholders this year for a series of strategic dialogues on the future of the European automotive industry. As a result, we now have the industrial action plan for the European automotive sector. What it does is it lays out what is needed to support the EU automotive industry's transition to clean, connected and increasingly automated vehicles and to make sure that they stay competitive globally.

Over the past months, several of these actions have been implemented and many others are coming up. Keep in mind, for example, the targeted amendment to the CO2 emissions standards for new cars and vans that we proposed in April of this year. It was adopted in record time, and it gives industry greater flexibility to comply with the target between 2025 to 2027, while leaving the overall targets unchanged. So, being pragmatic on the how and, we feel, it would also make sense to stay the course on the why and the what.

We do need to decarbonise the sector, as emissions from the overall transport sector are still above the levels of 30 years ago. We are doing it because it makes sense, not only for our climate goals, but also for our economic security and geopolitical reasons. Global players are speeding up investment, and more than one out of five cars sold in 2024 globally was electric. That is why we have set a clear framework for the transition to zero-emission vehicles with a clear target by 2035. This creates certainty for manufacturers and investors, while also providing enough time to plan a fair transition.

The objective is clear. Therefore, we should also aim to find a smart and workable way forward in the context of the review of the CO2 standard regulations. During the third strategic dialogue in September, we confirmed we will speed up work to come forward with the foreseen review by the end of this calendar year. We've launched a call for evidence and public consultation, which will close this week. We will carefully assess all inputs received as we are preparing the impact assessment.

In that context, the Commission will continue to commit to the principle of technological neutrality. The impact assessment will look into all main technology developments, with their potential, with their challenges â be it plug-in hybrids, range extenders, e-fuels and other types of renewable fuels. As set out in the action plan, we also intend to come forward still this year with a proposal to decarbonise corporate fleets. Experiences from Member States have shown this to be a promising path to accelerate the transition to zero-emission vehicles.

The Commission will also soon come with a 'battery booster', with the aim to achieve near-term cost competitiveness of domestically produced cells and, of course, components. As announced by the President in her State of the Union address, we are preparing an initiative on small and affordable electric cars. Clean mobility, in our view, should be within reach for everyone.

Meanwhile, we will keep engaging with industry and with other stakeholders to monitor the situation and identify further strategic needs. In our view, with the right policy framework in place ensuring we deliver on the necessary enabling conditions and with such innovative and committed companies here in Europe, we are confident there is, as there should be, a thriving future for this hugely important sector.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Jens Gieseke, im Namen der PPE-Fraktion. â Frau PrÃ¤sidentin, sehr geehrter Herr Kommissar Hoekstra, liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen! Die europÃ¤ische Automobilindustrie steckt tief in der Krise. Beinahe wÃ¶chentlich hÃ¶ren wir von neuen EntlassungsankÃ¼ndigungen bei Herstellern und Zulieferern. Allein in Deutschland waren es in diesem Jahr Ã¼ber 51Â 500 Menschen, die entlassen worden sind. Die HÃ¼tte brennt also, aber anscheinend haben das hier immer noch nicht alle begriffen. Statt der Autoindustrie endlich die dringend notwendige UnterstÃ¼tzung zu geben, verlieren sich die rechten Fraktionen im puren Hass auf EâAutos. JÃ¼ngstes Beispiel: die eben erfolgte komplette Blockade bei der Mautbefreiung fÃ¼r EâLkw. Gleichzeitig klammern sich linke Fraktionen blind an eine reine Elektroideologie.

Liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen, als EVP scheinen wir verstanden zu haben, wie prekÃ¤r die Lage im Land wirklich ist, wie sich die Leute am Band fÃ¼hlen und die Ingenieure in den BÃ¼ros. Die Zukunft ist nicht rein elektrisch, und sie ist auch nicht rein der VerbrennerÂ â das ist vÃ¶llig klar. Wir brauchen wieder Vernunft und Realismus in unserer Politik. Wir brauchen einen Technologiemix. Wir brauchen eine technologische Offenheit. Unsere Automobilindustrie braucht jetzt dringend UnterstÃ¼tzung. Wir mÃ¼ssen den Hochlauf der ElektromobilitÃ¤t fÃ¶rdern, ja, die Infrastruktur ausbauen, die Standortbedingungen verbessern.

Es braucht aber auch dringend einen realistischen Plan zur Dekarbonisierung. DafÃ¼r brauchen wir einen Vorschlag der Kommission. Ursula von der Leyen hat es angekÃ¼ndigt, und ich bin froh, dass Kommissar Hoekstra gerade angesprochen hat, dass auch neue Technologien wie PlugâinâHybride und range extender eine Rolle spielen werden, ebenso wie nachhaltige Kraftstoffe. Wenn wir Klimaschutz und WettbewerbsfÃ¤higkeit vereinen wollen, dann brauchen wir alle zur VerfÃ¼gung stehenden Technologien. Ansonsten werden wir diese Industrie in Europa verlieren.

Liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen, wir werden zum Jahresende wohl ein Weihnachtsgeschenk von der Kommission bekommen. Als EVP sind wir bereit, in der Mitte dieses Hauses zu arbeiten â das betone ich ausdrÃ¼cklich. Lassen Sie uns das gemeinsam anpacken in der MitteÂ â fÃ¼r ArbeitsplÃ¤tze, fÃ¼r Wohlstand und fÃ¼r eine Zukunft des europÃ¤ischen Automobilstandorts.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Mohammed Chahim, on behalf of the S&D Group. â Madam President, Europe is facing a Kodak moment. Back in the 1970s, Kodak invented the digital camera â a groundbreaking technology â but they feared it would destroy the business model of cameras and film. So they delayed, they resisted and they have practically vanished.

Today, our automotive industry stands at the same crossroad, and history is clear: horse and carriage lost to the steam engine; the steam engine lost to the combustion car; and the combustion engine will be, for a big part, replaced by electric vehicles.

Instead of focusing on meeting of the current standard emissions, some start delaying with range extenders, very expensive synthetic fuels and other distractions. That's not innovation; that's sabotage. Because around the world, others are moving ahead: 42Â % of new cars in Vietnam are electric; Ethiopia has already stopped importing combustion engines; and Chinese EV sales grew 40Â % last year.

We owe Europe honesty and ambition. This debate today is not only misguided, it's also dishonest. We need to wake up and smell the coffee. Choosing for short-term profits will go at the expense of long-term gains for our auto industry, for cars being built in Europe.

Let's instead invest in electric corporate fleets, in social leasing programmes, so that the nurse, the teacher, the farmer can all drive electric cars, and not just the consultant, the lawyer and the banker.

Otherwise, I sincerely believe our car industry risks ending up like Kodak: as a mere snapshot of history.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Klara Dostalova, za skupinu PfE. â PanÃ­ pÅedsedajÃ­cÃ­, pane komisaÅi, kdysi bylo evropskÃ© auto symbolem sÃ­ly, prosperity a vynalÃ©zavosti. Bylo to naÅ¡e zlato, naÅ¡e pÃ½cha, naÅ¡e Å¾ivobytÃ­. Miliony lidÃ­ po celÃ© EvropÄ pracovaly v tovÃ¡rnÃ¡ch, v dodavatelskÃ½ch firmÃ¡ch, v servisech, celÃ© regiony Å¾ily z automobilovÃ©ho prÅ¯myslu. A dnes â kvÅ¯li EvropskÃ© komisi â jsme svÄdky jeho postupnÃ© likvidace.

Komise se rozhodla, Å¾e vÃ­ nejlÃ©pe, co je pro nÃ¡s dobrÃ©. A tak jednoho dne z tepla svÃ½ch kancelÃ¡ÅÃ­ pÅiÅ¡la s velkolepÃ½m zÃ¡kazem aut se spalovacÃ­mi motory od roku 2035. Bez plÃ¡nÅ¯, bez debaty, bez rozumu. Bylo jim jedno, co to udÄlÃ¡ s pracovnÃ­mi mÃ­sty. Bylo jim jedno, co to udÄlÃ¡ s cenami aut. Bylo jim jedno, jestli na to mÃ¡me infrastrukturu. My jsme ale varovali. ZamÄstnanci varovali odbory, varovali dodavatele, varovali celÃ© regiony, kÅiÄeli: âNedÄlejte to, zniÄÃ­te nÃ¡m Å¾ivoty.â A co udÄlala Komise? SmÃ¡la se nÃ¡m do oÄÃ­. OznaÄovala nÃ¡s za zpÃ¡teÄnÃ­ky, nevzdÄlance, za ty, kdo brÃ¡nÃ­ pokroku. ZkrÃ¡tka arogance, pohrdÃ¡nÃ­, vÃ½smÄch.

A teÄ? TeÄ se jejich plÃ¡n hroutÃ­ jako domeÄek z karet. Prodeje elektromobilÅ¯ padajÃ­, protoÅ¾e lidÃ© si je prostÄ nemohou dovolit. VÃ½roba aut v EvropÄ klesÃ¡, dodavatelskÃ© ÅetÄzce ovlÃ¡dÃ¡ ÄÃ­na, kterÃ¡ si u nÃ¡s stavÃ­ tovÃ¡rny a pÅivÃ¡Å¾Ã­ si vlastnÃ­ dÄlnÃ­ky. A tisÃ­ce naÅ¡ich lidÃ­ mezitÃ­m konÄÃ­ na ÃºÅadech prÃ¡ce.

A co na to Komise? No, jako vÅ¾dycky zaklÃ¡dÃ¡ novÃ© strategie, novÃ© pracovnÃ­ skupiny, novÃ© kulatÃ© stoly, dalÅ¡Ã­ papÃ­ry, dalÅ¡Ã­ prezentace, tiskovÃ© konference. To je jejich lÃ©k na kaÅ¾dou krizi. Ale strategie ÃºÄty nezaplatÃ­. KolegovÃ©, nedovolme zniÄit automobilovÃ½ prÅ¯mysl.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Carlo Fidanza, a nome del gruppo ECR. â Signora Presidente, onorevoli colleghi, signor Commissario, oggi l'industria automobilistica, il cuore pulsante della nostra economia, rischia di morire soffocata da scelte ideologiche e da una visione tecnocratica che non tengono conto della realtÃ . Il bando ai motori a combustione dal 2035 Ã¨ l'emblema di questo tragico errore.

Non Ã¨ una politica ambientale, Ã¨ una politica industriale sbagliata e suicida che sta consegnando alla Cina il quasi monopolio sul mercato e il controllo di intere filiere produttive, con l'elettrico che diventa il cavallo di Troia cinese per conquistare anche il mercato endotermico.

Intanto, centinaia di migliaia di posti di lavoro in tutta Europa rischiano di sparire. E la Commissione cosa fa? Perde tempo in un dialogo strategico in cui sente ma non ascolta il grido di dolore che viene dal settore, in particolare dallo straordinario tessuto di piccole e medie industrie dell'indotto e della componentistica che rischiano di sparire. Rinvia le multe ai produttori, sÃ¬, ma soltanto per auto e furgoni e, incredibilmente, non per i veicoli pesanti.

E la ricetta magica della Presidente von der Leyen per fare concorrenza ai cinesi sarebbe produrre in Europa mini auto elettriche a basso costo. Un'idea ridicola che suona come la resa definitiva di un continente che ha costruito la sua grandezza sull'innovazione e sull'industria di qualitÃ .

Ecco perchÃ© la lettera congiunta di Italia e Germania alla Commissione segna un punto di svolta. Due grandi paesi manifatturieri chiedono con forza di cambiare rotta, di riscrivere le regole, di superare i dogmi del 2035, del "tutto elettrico", rimettendo al centro neutralitÃ  tecnologica e libertÃ  di innovazione.

Serve una politica industriale europea che riconosca il valore dei carburanti rinnovabili, dei biocarburanti, degliÂ e-fuel, dei motori ibridi e delle nuove soluzioni a combustione pulita. Serve una politica che difenda la sovranitÃ  tecnologica, produttiva e strategica dell'Europa, invece di consegnarci mani e piedi ai concorrenti asiatici.

Voglio fare per questo un appello agli amici del PPE. Su questi temi c'Ã¨ una maggioranza possibile in quest'Aula. Lasciamo che si esprima con un voto, senza subire il perenne ricatto della sinistra, nemica dell'industria, del lavoro e dell'ambiente.

E c'Ã¨ una maggioranza anche in Consiglio se, come ci auguriamo, il cancelliere Merz sarÃ  capace di tenere a freno i suoi alleati di governo.

Noi lo diciamo da tempo: la sostenibilitÃ  non nasce dai divieti, ma dalla libertÃ  di innovare e dal coraggio di competere. Per questo revocare il bando del 2035 non Ã¨ un passo indietro, Ã¨ un atto di luciditÃ  politica per salvare un settore strategico, milioni di lavoratori e l'indipendenza industriale del nostro continente.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Gerben-Jan Gerbrandy, on behalf of the Renew Group. â Madam President, colleagues, 2035 is ten years from now and we will be three US presidents further, and obviously we still have President von der Leyen sitting here. But if you look ten years forward, it is also very valuable to look ten years back as well.Â In 2015, the best selling electric vehicle was the European Renault Zoe with a range of 100Â km. Today's EV world is incomparable to that of 2015, and the 2035 EV world will be equally incomparable to today's.

President von der Leyen said in her State of the Union speech that 'the future is electric,' and I fully agree. The big question is: will this electric future be European or Chinese? I want it to be European â no, it needs to be European. Therefore the title of this debate should have been 'how do we make our electric future European'? What does the car industry need from us to make the transition successful, and what should they do themselves?

In history, better technologies have always replaced the worse. Whether you like it or not. In the 18thÂ century, the Luddites were convinced they could stop progress by destroying automated machinery in the textile industry. Obviously, they couldn't. Let us not be the 21stÂ century Luddites trying to destroy the European car industry. Let us do what made Europe great: embrace new technology and develop it in such a way that we lead the world.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Michael Bloss, im Namen der Verts/ALE-Fraktion. â Frau PrÃ¤sidentin, Herr Kommissar, liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen! Reden wir mal ehrlich: China ist der grÃ¶Ãte Neuwagenmarkt der Welt, und der elektrifiziert im Eiltempo. Und dort verlieren europÃ¤ische Hersteller jeden Tag Marktanteile. Die Zahlen vom August: BMW minus 13Â Prozent, Audi minus 9Â Prozent und Mercedes-Benz minus 20Â Prozent. Das ist der Kern des Problems. Unsere Industrie schwÃ¤chelt nicht, weil wir bei der ElektromobilitÃ¤t zu schnell waren. Sie schwÃ¤chelt, weil andere schneller investieren.

Und hÃ¶ren Sie auf, die ElektromobilitÃ¤t schlechtzureden. Ich habe gestern das Mercedes-Benz-Werk in Rastatt besucht. Da wird der neue elektrische CLA gebaut, und der geht weg wie warme Semmeln. Die mÃ¼ssen jetzt auf Dreischichtbetrieb umstellen, weil sich das Ding so gut verkauft und sie mit der Produktion nicht hinterherkommen. E-Autos sind einfach richtig gute Autos, und jeder weiÃ, dass sie die Zukunft sind. Deswegen investiert China Milliarden, und Sie diskutieren, ob es richtig ist, den RÃ¼ckwÃ¤rtsgang einzulegen. Wenn Sie die fossilen Verbrenner bis in alle Ewigkeit fahren wollen und die Klimaziele infrage stellen, dann zerstÃ¶ren Sie das Fundament erfolgreicher Wirtschaftspolitik. Dann gibt es keine Investitions- und Planungssicherheit. Es ist doch klar: In einem Rennen, in dem man zurÃ¼ckliegt, da bremst man nicht. Man beschleunigt, um zu gewinnen. Und wir GrÃ¼nen, wir wollen, dass Europa dieses Rennen gewinnt.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Merja KyllÃ¶nen, on behalf of The Left Group. â Madam President, dear colleagues, Europe's automotive industry stands at a crossroad.Â Our goal â to achieve climate neutrality â is beyond question. But the path we choose must be guided by reason, innovation and fairness.

A blanket ban on internal combustion engines risks doing the opposite. It penalises technologies rather than emissions, discourages investment in new synthetic and renewable fuels, and weakens the global competitiveness of Europe's car industry â an industry that employs millions and drives much of our research and exports.

True progress comes not from banning technologies, but from unleashing innovation across all technologies that reduce carbon. Electric mobility is essential, yes â but so are low-carbon fuels, hydrogen hybrid solutions, new fuels and continuous efficiency improvements.

Climate policy should set targets, not dictate tools. A technologically neutral approach keeps Europe at the forefront of clean mobility. Let's lead with innovation, not prohibition.

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

IN THE CHAIR: JAVI LÃPEZ
Vice-President

Â Â Tiemo WÃ¶lken (S&D). â Herr PrÃ¤sident, liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen! Wir machen doch in der politischen Debatte gerade den gleichen Fehler, den fÃ¼hrende Automobilmanager in den letzten Jahren gemacht haben und damit Jobs vernichtet haben: Wir drehen uns in dieser Debatte im Kreis. Der europÃ¤ischen Automobilindustrie und den Zulieferern geht es schlecht, ja. Aber wir mÃ¼ssen die Frage beantworten: Warum? Weil Autos produziert werden, die im Massensegment des internationalen Marktes im Moment nicht mehr konkurrenzfÃ¤hig sind. Wir bauen groÃe, teure, schwere, zweifellos tolle Autos, aber es sind eben auch Autos, die auf 500Â Kilometer Fahrstrecke mal eben 75Â Euro Brennstoff verbrennen. Gekauft werden aber weltweit kleine, praktische, bezahlbare Fahrzeuge mit Elektromotor, die auf 500Â Kilometern 20Â Euro kosten. Man muss nicht Volkswirt sein, um zu verstehen, dass hier eine klare LÃ¼cke zwischen der Nachfrage und dem Angebot klafft.

Aber anstatt konsequent an der Seite der Autobauer zu stehen und diese LÃ¼cke zu schlieÃen und LÃ¶sungen fÃ¼r die gesamte WertschÃ¶pfungskette inklusive der Zulieferer zu finden, sind hier einige im Raum, die die Augen verschlieÃen und sagen: Ich will es aber so, wie es vor 40Â Jahren gewesen ist, wiederhaben. Aber so funktioniert das doch nicht. Deswegen mÃ¼ssen wir sagen: FÃ¼r wen arbeiten wir eigentlich? FÃ¼r Arbeitnehmerinnen und Arbeitnehmer, fÃ¼r einen wichtigen Industriezweig, fÃ¼r Menschen, die sich Fortbewegung leisten wollen, fÃ¼r die zukÃ¼nftige Generation. Deswegen mÃ¼ssen wir raus aus der Endlosschleife und endlich aktive Industriepolitik betreiben, liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen.

Â Â Jean-Paul Garraud (PfE). â Monsieur le PrÃ©sident, en interdisant la vente des vÃ©hicules thermiques Ã  partir deÂ 2035, l'Union europÃ©enne a signÃ© l'une de ses lÃ©gislations les plus absurdes et les plus suicidaires.

Sous couvert d'une transition Ã©cologique dogmatique, Bruxelles offre sur un plateau le marchÃ© europÃ©en de l'automobile aux constructeurs chinois et amÃ©ricains. Pendant que ces derniers protÃ¨gent jalousement leur industrie, nous sacrifions la nÃ´tre. Les gÃ©ants asiatiques produisent des voitures Ã©lectriques Ã  bas coÃ»t, souvent subventionnÃ©es par des Ãtats peu regardants sur l'Ã©cologie rÃ©elle et le droit du travail. RÃ©sultat: les entreprises europÃ©ennes ne peuvent pas tenir la cadence, leurs usines ferment, leurs ouvriers sont licenciÃ©s, leurs brevets fuient. Dans ce domaine, comme dans tant d'autres, le rÃªve europÃ©en devient un cauchemar industriel.

L'Association des constructeurs europÃ©ens d'automobiles, qui reprÃ©sente quatorzeÂ entreprises automobiles dans l'Union europÃ©enne, dont le fleuron franÃ§ais Renault, ne cesse de tirer la sonnette d'alarme. Le chancelier allemand s'est prononcÃ© lundi en faveur d'une rÃ©vision de cette interdiction, comprenant bien les dÃ©fis majeurs auxquels fait face l'industrie automobile.

Que dire des automobilistes, notamment de ceux qui vivent dans nos campagnes ou dans le pÃ©riurbain? Tous n'ont pas les moyens d'acheter un vÃ©hicule Ã©lectrique. La voiture Ã  moteur thermique reste leur seule option viable financiÃ¨rement et technologiquement. Demain, ces automobilistes seront mis au ban de la sociÃ©tÃ©, paupÃ©risÃ©s et privÃ©s d'une libertÃ© essentielle: celle de circuler.

Cette politique de l'Union europÃ©enne, dÃ©cidÃ©e par des technocrates hors sol, est brutale, injuste et mÃ©prise les rÃ©alitÃ©s sociales. Le Rassemblement national s'y oppose avec force. Nous exigeons le maintien de la libertÃ© des choix technologiques et la protection de notre industrie. L'Ã©cologie dogmatique ne doit pas Ãªtre un prÃ©texte Ã  la dÃ©construction de notre Ã©conomie.

Za prvÃ©, nesmyslnÃ½ zÃ¡kaz vÃ½roby aut se spalovacÃ­mi motory prostÄ hoÄte do koÅ¡e. Za druhÃ©, upravte nejenom emisnÃ­ cÃ­l pro rok 2035, ale taky pro rok 2030.Â Za tÅetÃ­, otevÅete dveÅe technologickÃ© neutralitÄ, hybridÅ¯m, syntetickÃ½m palivÅ¯m i pokroÄilÃ½m biopalivÅ¯m. Za ÄtvrtÃ©, nastartujte skuteÄnÃ½ rozvoj vodÃ­ku v EU, aby mohl bÃ½t efektivnÄ vyuÅ¾it takÃ© v dopravÄ. No a za pÃ¡tÃ© â a hlavnÄ â zeptejte se lidÃ­, co opravdu chtÄjÃ­. Dejte jim svobodu, jakÃ© auto si koupÃ­, aniÅ¾ by je to zruinovalo.

Pane komisaÅi, sundejte si klapky z oÄÃ­. ZaÄnÄte pracovat ve prospÄch naÅ¡ich obÄanÅ¯, ve prospÄch naÅ¡eho prÅ¯myslu.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Jan-Christoph Oetjen (Renew). â Herr PrÃ¤sident, Herr Kommissar, verehrte Kolleginnen und Kollegen! Die Automobilindustrie ist in der Krise, und ich glaube, wir sind uns einig, dass wir schnell handeln mÃ¼ssen. Und als Freie Demokraten kÃ¤mpfen wir schon lange dafÃ¼r, dass wir alle Technologien nutzen, um die Dekarbonisierung in Europa voranzubringen Und andere Teile der Welt, auch China Ã¼brigens, setzen dabei auch auf hybride LÃ¶sungen, auf range extender und auf alternative Kraftstoffe. Und ich bin der Meinung, wir sollten das auch in Europa tun und dafÃ¼r die CO2-Standardregulierung anpassen, sehr geehrter Herr Kommissar.

Ich mÃ¶chte hier betonen, dass sich das ausdrÃ¼cklich nicht gegen ElektromobilitÃ¤t richtet. Ganz im Gegenteil: Ich stimme Kommissar Hoekstra zu, dass wir alles dafÃ¼r tun mÃ¼ssen, um im Bereich der ElektromobilitÃ¤t und im Bereich des autonomen Fahrens federfÃ¼hrend zu sein auf der Welt. Und deswegen ist Handeln jetzt dringend notwendig. Aber um die Besten zu sein, verehrte Kolleginnen und Kollegen, und weil Sie die Dienstwagenregelung angesprochen haben, Herr Hoekstra: Ich glaube nicht, dass Zwang gegenÃ¼ber Menschen der richtige Weg dabei ist, sondern wir sollten Anreize setzen dafÃ¼r, dass alternative Kraftstoffe, dass hybride LÃ¶sungen, dass Elektrofahrzeuge genutzt werden, und nicht die Menschen zu ihrem GlÃ¼ck zwingen.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Virginijus SinkeviÄius (Verts/ALE). â Mr President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, nobody disputes the importance of the automotive industry, which directly and indirectly employs more than 13Â million people â not only in Germany, France, Spain or Italy, but all across Europe. However, the worst would be if our automotive industry has a Nokia moment, where at some point it has almost 50Â % of the market share and then it just diminishes. The competition is not coming in the combustion engine; nobody cares and nobody competes with European industry on combustion engine. Nobody wants to do a better turbodiesel or whatever. The future of automotive â like it or not â lays in battery and software. As simple as that.

What we must do to truly protect our industry is to ensure that we secure supply chains, first of all, and we are waiting for an important battery booster proposal from the Commission. Secondly, we need cheap energy; we cannot compete with expensive energy, but it was a mistake basing our energy mix in Russian hands. Thirdly, we need to ensure that we have a workforce, a workforce that is future-oriented and able to compete. And finally, if we want an uptake from the market, we cannot fail our people. There must be, first of all, infrastructure â which is not there, we are far behind the goals â and lastly, cheap energy. That will help our industry.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Rudi Kennes (The Left). â Voorzitter, aan de rechterzijde van dit Parlement horen we dat het toekomstige verbod op autoâs met een verbrandingsmotor moet worden teruggedraaid. Dit zou zogenaamd nodig zijn om de Europese productie en Europese banen te redden. Maar niets is minder waar. Deze ommekeer dient slechts om de kortetermijnkwartaalwinsten van de automobielindustrie veilig te stellen â een industrie die bovendien hopeloos te laat was met investeren in groene productie en daardoor de boot heeft gemist.

In plaats van te investeren in goedkope elektrische autoâs, bleven zij massaal inzetten op zware, vervuilende autoâs. Tegelijkertijd verplaatsten zij hun productie massaal naar landen waar werknemers nauwelijks betaald worden en weinig tot geen rechten hebben.

Moeten de Europese automobielarbeiders de prijs betalen, nu blijkt dat deze industrie helemaal niet klaar is voor de toekomst? Zeker weten van niet. We mogen ons niet laten misleiden door de leugens van rechts. De Europese automobielarbeiders hebben niets aan een autoâindustrie die met volle snelheid op de afgrond afstevent. Zij hebben behoefte aan een industrie die klaar is voor de toekomst.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Markus Buchheit (ESN). â Herr PrÃ¤sident, meine sehr verehrten Damen und Herren! 2019, als der Green Deal gerade in den StartlÃ¶chern stand und als Greta Thunberg hier noch die Flure durchschritt, da hatten wir bereits Studien, die sagten, dass wir in den nÃ¤chsten Jahren alleine in Deutschland 200Â 000 bis 400Â 000Â ArbeitsplÃ¤tze in der Automobilindustrie verlieren wÃ¼rden, wenn das Verbrennerverbot kÃ¤me. Nun stehen wir heute wieder hier, und es wird die Frage gestellt: Mensch, der Industrie geht es so schlecht, wir wissen gar nicht, warum es ihr so schlecht geht.

Herr Kollege WÃ¶lken von der SPD: Es geht der Industrie deswegen schlecht, weil Leute wie Sie eine aktivierende Industriepolitik vertreten wollen, und das heiÃt nichts anderes âÂ die Zuschauer mÃ¶gen sich mal das Video von vorher anschauen vom Kollegen WÃ¶lken â wie eine Planwirtschaft. Sie wollen eingreifen bis hin zum Produktionsmanagement einzelner Firmen, und das kann es ja wohl nicht sein. Wenn hier das Beispiel angebracht wird von iPhones und dem Durchsetzen gegenÃ¼ber Nokia, von der Pferdekutsche und dem KÃ¼rzertreten gegenÃ¼ber dem Automobil, dann kann all denen nur gesagt werden: Diese Projekte, diese Technologien haben sich durchgesetzt â von freien Unternehmen geplant, von freien BÃ¼rgern nachgefragt.

Was wir brauchen, ist Freiheit, nicht noch mehr Planwirtschaft, nicht noch mehr WÃ¶lkens in diesem Hause hier. Wir brauchen wieder Freiheit fÃ¼r unsere Unternehmen und fÃ¼r unsere BÃ¼rger. In diesem Sinne: Weg mit diesem Verbot, aber auch weg mit den Flottenstrafzahlungen!

Â

Â Â Peter Liese (PPE). â Herr PrÃ¤sident, liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen! Einige tun so, als sei das Verbrennerverbot der Kern der europÃ¤ischen Klimapolitik. Das stimmt nicht. Nein, nein, nein, das stimmt nicht. Allein der Emissionshandel bringt 25Â Mal so viel. Selbst wenn wir die CO2-Gesetzgebung fÃ¼rÂ Pkw komplett abschaffen wÃ¼rden, dann wÃ¤re das nur ein geringer Teil. Aber ich will sie gar nicht komplett abschaffen. Ich will TechnologieneutralitÃ¤t, und dann hat das Ã¼berhaupt keinen Einfluss auf das Erreichen unserer Klimaziele.

Aber auch die andere Seite hat unrecht. Es ist nicht so, dass die Krise der europÃ¤ischen Autoindustrie total oder auch nur hauptsÃ¤chlich durch europÃ¤ische Gesetzgebung bedingt ist. Weder die Managementfehler von VW noch die Fehler der Ampelregierung in DeutschlandÂ â Streichung von UnterstÃ¼tzung Ã¼ber NachtÂ â noch, dass die Chinesen einfach jetzt eine eigene Autoindustrie haben und nicht mehr europÃ¤ische Autos kaufen, kann man doch der EuropÃ¤ischen Union und unserer Gesetzgebung in die Schuhe schieben.

Deswegen mÃ¼ssen wir dieses leidige Thema endlich aus der Welt schaffen. Die Leute sind genervt von endlosen Kontroversen. Wir wollen TechnologieneutralitÃ¤t, aber wir wollen auch KlimaneutralitÃ¤t. Und das geht! Den Partnern in Berlin, den Koalitionsparteien rufe ich zu: Einigt euch! Die Leute sind genervt von dem Streit. Und uns rufe ich zuÂ â allen Fraktionen, vor allen Dingen denen in der Mitte: Einigen wir uns, die Leute sind genervt von unserem Streit! Machen wir endlich eine Regelung zur TechnologieneutralitÃ¤t und zur KlimaneutralitÃ¤t. Dann ist das Thema weg, und wir kÃ¶nnen uns auf die wirklichen Probleme konzentrieren.

Â 
Â Â

Â Â Johan Danielsson (S&D). â Mr President, at its core, this debate is about the fact that large parts of this House do not believe in climate change or change at all.

During the election campaign, one of your Swedish ECR colleagues had the opportunity to explain why we shouldn't worry about climate change. He explained â and listen carefully now â that we should not worry, because if we hadn't had climate change, we would still have dinosaurs. And even if it's cute when older men worry about dinosaurs, it is fundamentally a serious issue, because we cannot allow the far right's fear of dinosaurs to threaten the green transition of European industry and ultimately our competitiveness.

Because in the end, the EU's 2035 target is not a threat to the car industry, it is an opportunity. We should focus on helping the car industry. We forced the rollout of sorting stations to make sure that we have worldâclass European battery production. We have social leasing schemes so ordinary people also can demand Europeanâmade electric cars. This is the way to defend the auto industry.

Â

Â Â Jorge BuxadÃ© Villalba (PfE). â SeÃ±or presidente, ayer El PaÃ­s, uno de los diarios espaÃ±oles mÃ¡s sectarios, decÃ­a que el auge de los Patriotas estaba poniendo fin a la electrificaciÃ³n del automÃ³vil. Pues miren, por supuesto, se lo digo: sÃ­. Vamos a poner fin a su fanÃ¡tica prohibiciÃ³n del motor de combustiÃ³n.

Pero ya no nos conformamos con eso. Miles de trabajadores despedidos en Alemania, en EspaÃ±a y en Italia quieren exigirles personalmente las responsabilidades a los que votaron a favor del Pacto Verde, a los que votaron a favor de la Directiva sobre los automÃ³viles, a los que votaron en contra de nuestra peticiÃ³n de derogaciÃ³n, a todos los medios y periodistas que aplaudieron la imposiciÃ³n del coche elÃ©ctrico y que criminalizan a los que defienden la libertad.

Que se declare responsables a sus partidos y a sus empresas y que les embarguen las sedes para venderlas y pagar las indemnizaciones de despido a los trabajadores: ese es nuestro sueÃ±o. Y en sus sedes haremos pisos baratos para espaÃ±oles. Muchos pisos y muy baratos. Los mejores pisos. Es el mejor plan de vivienda. Que ustedes, que son los responsables, paguen.

Miren, renunciar a una industria en que Ã©ramos lÃ­deres mundiales para ser dependientes de China, que produce el 70Â % de las baterÃ­as y controla toda la cadena de suministro, es la mejor forma de inducir al suicidio a la economÃ­a europea. Ustedes lo sabÃ­an y lo hicieron. Cientos de miles de despidos, empresas cerradas, fÃ¡bricas que suspenden turnos, el sector auxiliar y de componentes en crisis total. Ustedes lo sabÃ­an y lo hicieron.

Vamos a poner fin a esa loca prohibiciÃ³n y a promover que ustedes respondan. Porque lo sabÃ­an, y a pesar de que lo sabÃ­an âo precisamente por esoâ lo hicieron y votaron a favor.
